Reference temperature from substation group analysis (Wrocław)¶

Objective: Compare Foreca temperatures with controller temperatures for the selected winter period (Jan 9, 2026) and evaluate whether aggregated controller temperatures can serve as a stable reference signal.¶

Total number of substations registered in CRM for Wroclaw.¶

6687

Wrocław Substations Used in Analysis¶

Only substations with coordinates were used for further spatial analysis.¶

Number of unique substations in the file: 6055

Wrocław substations in CRM: 6687
Wrocław substations with coordinates: 6055
Missing coordinates: 632

Data Cleaning and Exclusions¶

OTZ substations (Wrocław only): 356
KIONA substations (Wrocław only): 45
Total excluded (Wrocław only): 399
Filtered Wrocław pool: 6055 -> 5656 (removed 399)

Data Quality Assessment Before Analysis¶

Physical Level — Summary¶

From 5656 Wrocław substations, 4 (0.07%) recorded physically impossible temperatures (< -40°C or > 60°C).¶

These substations are marked for exclusion at the physical filtering stage.¶

APPLYING PHYSICAL FILTER TO WROCŁAW POOL
Wrocław pool before physical filter: 5656
Removed at physical level: 4
Wrocław pool after physical filter: 5652
Share removed at physical level: 0.07 %

Physical Filtering — Example¶

Example of a substation excluded due to physically impossible controller temperature values.

Logical Filtering — Summary¶

78 substations (1.38%) were excluded at the logical level due to abnormal temperature bias behaviour (fence-based and sunny anomalies).¶

The remaining clean pool contains 5574 substations.¶

LOGICAL LEVEL REPORT
Wrocław pool before logical filter: 5652
Substations removed at logical level: 78
Share removed at logical level: 1.38 %
Wrocław pool after logical filter: 5574

Controller vs Foreca Bias Analysis (Final Clean Pool)¶

Controller vs Foreca deviation is computed using ±30 min anchor window for all substations that passed physical and logical filtering.

foreca_9 size: 48
ctrl_df size: 361179
Unique foreca timestamps: 48

Technical Summary (data coverage)¶

Active substations with matched Controller–Foreca data: 3510
Controller–Foreca comparison records used for analysis: 91156
Controller minus Foreca temperature range: -22.1 to 12.71

Controller vs Foreca Bias Analysis - Final Clean Pool¶

The following analysis evaluates temperature bias behaviour for substations that passed all filtering stages (OTZ/KIONA removal, physical validation, and logical anomaly exclusion).

Record-level distribution of all Controller - Foreca differences¶

Node-level bias ranking (mean delta per substation)¶

Distribution of mean bias across substations¶

The boxplot illustrates the distribution of mean temperature bias across substations. The median bias is clearly positive (~2°C), indicating that controller readings systematically exceed Foreca estimates. Most substations exhibit moderate bias, with a small number of higher positive and negative outliers.

Logical-level filtering (IQR-based)¶

Substations with extreme mean bias values were removed using an IQR-based method.

Lower bound: -3.735°C
Upper bound: 5.025°C

True-sunny substations were also excluded. Resulting clean pool size: 5488 substations.

Anomalous substations loaded (unique): 158
WRO pool (after OTZ/KIONA): 5652
Anomalous substations inside WRO pool: 78
Rows: before -> after: 5652 -> 5574 (removed 78 )
Final clean pool size: 5574

Geographically Distributed Sample (100 Substations)¶

The map highlights 100 substations selected using a 10×10 spatial grid to ensure representative coverage across Wrocław.

Geolocated substations total: 5574
Geolocated substations with valid Controller–Foreca data: 5574
Final selected substations: 100

Representative Substations by Deviation Level¶

Three representative substations were selected from the full clean pool (after all filtering) based on aggregated mean bias across the analysed period (mean Δ):

  • Substation with bias closest to zero
  • Substation with typical (median) bias
  • Substation with largest absolute bias (strongest deviation)
['907739', '903022', '911409']

Three representative substations were selected based on aggregated mean bias across the analysed period: closest to zero, median bias, and largest absolute deviation.

substation_id mean_delta
2851 907739 -0.000231
1298 903022 1.528846
3211 911409 8.136538

Mean Temperature Deviation Distribution¶

To evaluate bias at the city level, we analyse:

  • distribution of mean bias across substations
  • proportion of substations within deviation ranges
  • average daily temperature curves (all substations vs grid sample)

Distribution of mean deviation¶

Note: Mean bias is calculated only for substations with valid controller–Foreca matches on the selected day (3369 out of 5488 clean substations).

Conclusion¶

The 100-substation geographically distributed sample shows an average positive bias of ~1.48°C¶

(controller vs forecast).¶

Mean bias: 1.48°C
Std deviation: 1.53°C
Conclusion:
Mean controller temperature ≈ Foreca + 1.5°C

Case Studies: Sunny and Extreme Deviation Substations¶

feat rows: 3510 | days used: 2

1. True Sunny Substation (with noon temperature hump)¶

True Sunny substations are identified using statistical detection of noon temperature hump patterns (top 5% by hump, post-drop and noon delta).

True sunny substations (with hump): 5

anchor_df size: 91156
noon_anchor size: 17529
ctrl_day size: 361179

Best True Sunny substations: 900277

2. Not Sunny – Maximum Controller Overheating¶

Not sunny – max overheating: 911409

3. Not Sunny – Maximum Controller Undercooling¶

Not sunny – max undercooling: 903448

Anchor Window Deviation Distribution¶

The chart shows the share of substations that had at least one anchor-window extreme deviation beyond the ±5.025°C / −3.735°C thresholds during the analyzed day.

Total substations with anchor data: 3510

Overheated substations (max controller-foreca > 5.025°C): 1085 (30.9%)
Undercooled substations (min controller-foreca < -3.735°C): 29 (0.8%)

During the analysed winter period, 30.9% of substations exhibited at least one extreme positive deviation (Controller − Foreca > 5.025°C), while only 0.8% showed extreme negative deviation (< −3.735°C).

This strong asymmetry reflects the overall positive system bias observed in the dataset, where controller temperatures systematically exceed Foreca estimates.

Extreme overheating events are therefore significantly more common than undercooling in the winter period.
